rationale: The largest failure class is SQLWriter wrong_result from unmapped question predicates and yes/no semantics inversion, plus no_sql clarification stalls; I add a decomposition strategy and a semantics-inversion pitfall to force every qualifier into SQL and proceed without asking. I strengthen SchemaLinker with a lookup/enum decode heuristic (frequency codes, publisher/gender lookups) so joins and human-readable filters aren't dropped, and add an orchestrator commit-and-cap rule to stop turn-budget exhaustion. These target the highest-leverage, highest-volume issues and reduce variance by replacing judgment calls with deterministic procedures.

prompt_patches.new_text:
Decompose EVERY question into its full list of predicates and qualifiers BEFORE writing SQL, and verify each maps to a specific column/condition or ORDER BY: nouns, adjectives ('potential', 'latest'), yes/no attributes, and every named filter. Cover multi-clause questions completely — answering only one part is the top wrong_result cause.

For yes/no or attribute questions, restate the truth condition explicitly and encode it, e.g. 'well-finished'/'not closed' -> ClosedDate IS NULL; return the boolean/computed value, not a raw column for the summarizer to interpret.

For ratios, averages-per-unit, and percentages: CAST numerator AS REAL and build numerator/denominator over the SAME filtered rows; percentages use 100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN cond THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*).

For 'top/highest/most' single-entity questions add explicit ORDER BY <metric> DESC LIMIT 1.

NEVER end a turn with a clarifying question. State your assumption inline (BIRD convention: 'code' = the literal code column; percentage = per-distinct-entity) and hand off SQL every time.

Pass exact column spellings from describe_table (casing/spaces); confirm ambiguous names against sample_rows before handoff.

When a selected/filtered column is a foreign key to a lookup or enum table, ALWAYS include that lookup/join table and report the code->label mapping so the writer returns human-readable values and filters on the decoded label, not the raw id. Example: account.frequency 'POPLATEK TYDNE'=weekly, 'POPLATEK MESICNE'=monthly, 'POPLATEK PO OBRATU'=per transaction; superhero.eye_colour_id/gender_id/publisher_id join to colour/gender/publisher for names like publisher_name='Dark Horse Comics', gender='Female'.

Call sample_rows on every column that will be filtered, and report observed value formats (case, codes, units) so filters match stored values exactly. If the question's word (e.g. 'weekly') differs from the stored code, decode it and give the writer the exact stored value.

Commit early: once a validated query executes and returns rows consistent with the question, hand to the Summarizer immediately — do not re-verify already-known facts (e.g. status codes) or re-run variants.

Cap exploratory SQL probes at 2. After that, finalize the most plausible validated query rather than continuing to explore, especially when near the turn budget. Prefer finalizing a plausible answer over indefinite verification.

Never let a run end with no executed SQL or with a clarifying question as the final answer; if the writer stalls, direct it to adopt the standard interpretation and proceed.

memory_upserts.text: For yes/no attribute questions, explicitly restate the truth condition before coding — do not return a raw column and let the summarizer guess. 'well-finished'/'resolved' post means never closed -> ClosedDate IS NULL (a NON-null ClosedDate means the opposite). Verify polarity so booleans are not inverted.

List every qualifier in the question and map each to SQL before finalizing. 'highest potential score for crossing' needs the crossing metric AND the 'potential' qualifier AND per-player latest-record dedup — not MAX(crossing) alone. Missing a qualifier or dedup is a wrong_result.

Decode enum/lookup codes for the writer instead of matching the question word literally: account.frequency 'POPLATEK TYDNE'=weekly, 'POPLATEK MESICNE'=monthly. Always include the lookup join table for FK id columns (colour/gender/publisher for superhero) so filters/outputs use labels, not ids.
